title: The Macro Minute:  Who’s right—President Trump or Fed Chair Powell?

Happy Thursday out there team42. It's
your skipper here at Darius Dell to
present our macro minute for Thursday,
May 8th, 2025. As always, we'll start
with the executive summary from today's
leadoff morning note. If you would like
to review the analysis supporting these
conclusions and to see if you need to
make any changes in your portfolio to
remain on the right side of market risk,
you need to be a client of 42 macro. So
today's macro question is who's right,
President Trump or Fed Chair Pal? Our
answer, President Trump's latest attack
on Fed Chair POW follows yesterday's Fed
decision at POW Presser in which the Fed
exercised continued caution regarding
further policy adjustments. The FOMC
statement concluded risk of higher
unemployment and higher inflation have
risen and the decision to leave policy
rates unchanged was unanimous. There
were a handful of statements that POW
made yesterday that indicated he is much
further along in his understanding of
the full distribution of probable
economic and financial market risks than
President Trump. All told, Pal's latest
commentary is consistent with our view
that the Fed should be doing absolutely
nothing right now to preserve foreign
investor confidence in the US dollar and
US assets. Any threat to central bank
independence, real or perceived, risk
reigniting capital outflows and another
round of the sell America trade. See
April 21st for more details. Moreover,
preemptively cutting rates right now
would likely be so counterproductive in
tolerance of above target inflation
signaling terms that it risks igniting a
sell-off in the bond market. See fall of
2024 for more details. Additionally,
increasingly unound fiscal policy means
credible monetary policy is the only
thing standing in the way between
current pricing in the Treasury bond
market and another 100 to 200 basis
points of term premium widening from
here. See April 11th for more details.
If Pal blinks and causes investors and
foreign creditors to broadly lose
confidence in both the US dollar and the
long end of the curve, his legacy will
be worse than Arthur Burns. At 72 years
old, Pal's worst fear must be that the
world remembers him for running
aggressively expansionary monetary
policy amid aggressively expansionary
fiscal policy for so long that it
triggered a 40-year high in inflation
and eventually spooked the bond market
so bad that perpetual yield curve
control became necessary. That'd be one
hell of a legacy, Mr. Pal. So hopefully
you're listening and and and kudos to
you for uh for uh for you know doing
what you got to do to to to preserve our
US economic exceptionalism and and
exorbitant privilege. Uh thank you sir
for your service. Uh transitioning to
our 42 micro dashboard as always to wrap
up with the question uh from our
community. Uh this one's regarded Fed
doing stealth QE. Uh any idea why this
hasn't gotten more attention? Seems to
be accurate based on these soma auction
purchases. So uh the this uh that the
client is referring to is this headline
here uh from uh what is this blockchain
news uh uh that's a website I guess is a
crypto website says Fed purchases 42
billion in 10-year bonds stealth QE
signals bullish momentum uh for Bitcoin
and crypto market. Uh boy, this is this
is exactly the kind of nonsense that I I

and snake oil salesman that operate in
this space and really by and large
operate in the space that sells research
and insights to retail investors. Uh

charlatans, and snake oil salesmen
telling retail investors exactly what
they need to hear or want to hear. ex

what the retail investors want to hear
so that the shields charlatan and snake
salesman continue to get paid based on
clicks flows
subscriptions hodddling Bitcoin and
making sure the price stays high or goes
up. All this stuff is designed to get
you guys to participate in the Ponzi.
Now, I'm not at all saying Bitcoin is a
Ponzi. Bitcoin is a real legitimate
institutional asset class that I have
believed in for five years now. I've
been a Bitcoiner. I'm a huge Bitcoiner.
I will always be a Bitcoiner, borrowing
some, you know, unforeseen events. Uh,
so I don't believe Bitcoin is a Ponzi. I
believe the constant marketing of
Bitcoin is the Ponzi. Getting more
people on on the ladder, convincing them
to huddle during draw downs to keep the
price higher than it otherwise would be
in a market cycle downturn. Uh, that's
the Ponzi. It's the p it's the it's the
infrastructure around Bitcoin that is
the Ponzi that you know guys that that
the shields charlatans and snake oil
salesmen are you know using to extract
revenue from you guys either through
advertising. I'm looking at this Adobe
advertisement right here. I'm sure if I

advertisements or subscri I'm sure these
people have a pro service. You can I see
a login so clearly login/join. how you
can buy their services, but then
ultimately your activity, you know,
inflates the value of not only Bitcoin,
but obviously other cryptos in the
market. And and so, uh, going back to
this question, the Fed is not doing
stealth QE. The Fed would signal if it
was doing QE because it would it would
stealth QE is is it would be a worse
version of doing QE. If you're going to
do QE, you'd rather signal to the market
that you're doing QE so that the policy
is more effective. But more importantly,
here's why the Fed isn't doing stealth
QE. This headline says the Fed um
purchases 42 billion in 10-year bonds. I
don't think people the average person
clearly and the operating this website
and and very clearly thinks the p
average reader of this website is too
dumb to realize that bonds mature like
if the if the Federal Reserve is trying
to roll off 5 billion a month of B bonds
on its balance sheet and let's say I
don't know 47 billion bonds matured that
month because there's a lot of bonds on
the Fed's balance sheet you know three
or four trillion dollars
worth then they need to buy 42 billion
to keep the balance sheet the same So
this is the whole point I'm making is
that this is just they're taking very
esoteric but but more importantly non
news news items and turning it into news
so that they can trigger your you know
the gullible reaction that you have to
either buy their services, buy more
crypto or buy more Bitcoin and
ultimately do things that may be
potentially beneficial for you or not
beneficial for you but will most
certainly be beneficial for them. And
this is the kind of charlatan snake oil
salesman type nonsense that uh you know
that we try to you know guard our
clients against and guard our community
against here. This is why one of the
many reasons why 42 macro exists is to
elevate the the intellect and the wisdom
um among retail investors so that
they're not being shilled to death by
nonsense like this. Again, the bonds
mature. And so the Fed doesn't want $47
billion of bonds to roll off its balance
sheet when it's targeting an explicit
roll out policy of 5 billion. It has to
buy 42 more billion with the maturing
bonds uh proceeds in order to, you know,
not prevent a faster uh um decay in its
balance sheet um than it otherwise would
be. If the Fed never bought bonds, then
its balance sheet would just shrink to
zero and it's, you know, bank reserves
would rise as bonds shrink to zero um
because the, you know, the Fed is not um
or the Fed's balance sheet would just
keep shrinking and as the as the bond
proceeds um mature, as the bonds matured
and the proceeds were doled back out.
So, um this is very basic stuff. I I
think anybody understanding who is in
institutional finance that understands
the S portfolio, it's nuts to me that I
even have to have a conversation about
this to be totally honest with you.
